messages.content: how to restrict access by IP in AWS Cloudfront

To restrict access by IP in AWS CloudFront, follow these steps:

1. Sign in to the AWS Management Console and navigate to the CloudFront console.

2. Select the distribution for which you want to restrict access by IP.

3. Select the "Behaviors" tab and choose the behavior you want to restrict.

4. Scroll down to the "Viewer Protocol Policy" section and select "Redirect HTTP to HTTPS" or "HTTPS Only" as per your requirement.

5. Go to the "Restrict Viewer Access (Use Signed URLs or Signed Cookies)" section and select "Yes" for "Restrict Viewer Access."

6. Choose "Whitelist" for the "Trusted Key Groups and/or Trusted Signers" option.

7. Select "IP Addresses" for the "Whitelist or Block Access by" option.

8. In the "Whitelist" text box, enter the allowed IP addresses separated by comma.

9. Save the changes and test the configuration.

Once this configuration is setup, CloudFront will only allow access to the content from the IP addresses that are specified in the whitelist.
AWS S3 replicate job with error message "srcgetaclnotpermitted"

The error message "srcgetaclnotpermitted" indicates that AWS S3 replication failed to retrieve the Access Control List (ACL) of the source object. This error occurs when the source object's ACL cannot be retrieved due to the lack of permissions of the IAM user or role that is used for replication.

To resolve the "srcgetaclnotpermitted" error, follow these steps:

1. Ensure that the IAM user or role that is used for S3 replication has the necessary permissions to access the source object's ACL. Specifically, the user should have the "s3:GetObjectAcl" permission.

2. Validate that the source S3 bucket is correctly configured to allow the IAM user or role to access the object's ACL. One way to do this is by granting the "s3:GetObjectAcl" permission to the relevant IAM users or roles at the bucket policy level.

3. Confirm that the source object being replicated exists, and that the user has permission to access it. Also, check that no other access control settings are interfering with object access permission.

4. Another possibility is that the object being replicated has already expired or has been deleted. In this scenario, the source object's ACL may no longer exist and that this error will happen. Check to ensure that the object still exists as required.

5. Finally, if none of the above actions resolved the error, ensure that you have the most up-to-date version of the S3 replication job. Update as necessary and re-run the job.

If none of the troubleshooting steps addressed the error, log a support ticket with Amazon Web Services support for further assistance.
How to configure IP based access control in AWS ALB

AWS Application Load Balancer (ALB) provides several options for IP-based access control through Security Groups and Network Access Control Lists (NACLs). Here’s how to configure IP-based access control in AWS ALB:

1. Configuring Security Groups: 

a. Sign in to the AWS Management Console and navigate to the EC2 console.
b. Click on the “Security Groups” option under the “NETWORK & SECURITY” tab.
c. Select the security group associated with your ALB.
d. Click on the “Inbound Rules” tab and then click on the “Edit” button.
e. Select “Custom TCP” from the protocol drop-down list.
f. In the “Port Range” field, enter the port number used by your ALB.
g. In the “Source” field, choose “My IP” or “Custom” and specify the IP addresses that are allowed to access the ALB.

2. Configuring Network Access Control Lists (NACLs):

a. Sign in to the AWS Management Console and navigate to the VPC console.
b. Click on the “Network ACLs” option under the “SECURITY” tab.
c. Select the NACL associated with your ALB.
d. Click on the “Inbound Rules” tab and then click on the “Edit” button.
e. Add a new rule to allow traffic from the specified IP addresses on the required port used by the ALB.
f. You can also set an Allow/Deny rule in the NACL to control the traffic between the source IP addresses and the ALB.

Note: While configuring the NACLs and security groups, ensure that you have allowed the required ports for the health checks, otherwise the ALB might return unhealthy instances from ELB.

Once the IP-based access control is configured, the ALB will only accept requests from the specified IP addresses. All other requests will be denied access.

Why do I get error "aws_event_loop_new_default_with_options+0x4a" when using AWSCRT

The error "aws_event_loop_new_default_with_options+0x4a" is a runtime error that can occur when using the AWS Common Runtime (AWSCRT) library due to a mismatch or inconsistency between the version of AWSCRT being used and the application that is built on top of it. 

To resolve this error, you should ensure that both the AWSCRT library version and the application that is utilizing it are the same and compatible. Here are the steps to try:

1. Check the version of the AWSCRT library that is being used by your application. Verify that the version of AWSCRT being used by your application is compatible with the version of your application being built.

2. Ensure that you have installed the correct version of the AWSCRT SDK and that your application is linking with the correct libraries that are a match for the version it was coded with.

3. Check that the AWSCRT library is built as per the instructions provided by AWS. When building the AWSCRT library from source code, make sure to follow the instructions carefully.

4. If the error persists, rebuild the application after updating it with the latest version of the AWSCRT libraries. Keeping all dependencies updated to their latest compatible versions is a good practice to avoid such errors.

5. If none of the above steps resolve the issue, reach out to AWS support for assistance, providing the specifics of the error including the context of the function or application that is encountering the error to help them more accurately diagnose and resolve the issue.
/usr/local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/_awscrt.cpython-310-x86_64-linux-gnu.so

"/usr/local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/_awscrt.cpython-310-x86_64-linux-gnu.so" is a shared object file that is generated when you install the Python AWS Common Runtime (AWSCRT) module on a Linux-based system. 

AWSCRT is a high-performance C library that is developed by Amazon for building scalable and high-performance client-server applications in AWS. It provides implementations of core AWS services and can be used with languages like Python, Java, Ruby, and C++.

"_awscrt.cpython-310-x86_64-linux-gnu.so" is the name of the native C extension that implements the Python bindings. When you run a Python script or application that utilizes the AWSCRT module, Python loads this native C extension.

The file is located in the "/usr/local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/" directory, which is a default path for installing third-party Python modules on a Linux-based system. When you install the AWSCRT Python module, the "_awscrt.cpython-310-x86_64-linux-gnu.so" file is installed in this directory.

If you encounter any issues with this file or AWSCRT module, you can try to reinstall the module, ensure you have the right version of the library, link your applications with the correct libraries, or contact AWS support for further assistance.
/usr/local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/_awscrt.cpython-310-x86_64-linux-gnu.so(aws_backtrace_print+0x4f) [0x7fac42d957ef]\

"/usr/local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/_awscrt.cpython-310-x86_64-linux-gnu.so(aws_backtrace_print+0x4f) [0x7fac42d957ef]" is a message that appears in a Python stack trace when an error occurs in the AWS Common Runtime (AWSCRT) module. 

When an error occurs, AWSCRT generates this stack trace to provide more information about the error and to help you diagnose the problem. The "_awscrt.cpython-310-x86_64-linux-gnu.so" file is the native C extension module that provides the Python bindings for AWSCRT.

"aws_backtrace_print+0x4f" is the name of the function within the AWSCRT library that is responsible for printing the stack trace. This function is useful for debugging and diagnosing runtime errors that occur in AWSCRT.

Here are some steps you can try to resolve the error:

1. Check if the AWSCRT module is installed correctly with all its dependencies. Ensure that you have installed the correct version of the AWSCRT SDK and that your application is linking with the correct libraries that are a match for the version it was coded with.

2. Use the stack trace to identify the function or module that is causing the error and review the documentation and reference resources to investigate the root cause of the error.

3. Check if there are any updates for the AWSCRT module and try upgrading the module to the latest version, which may contain bug fixes or improved functionality.

4. If the issue persists, it is recommended to reach out to the AWS support team, specifying the specifics of the error, including the context of your application that is running the Python code, and the version of the AWSCRT Python module. AWS support can provide further assistance or help you escalate the issue to AWS developers or engineers.
what is assert

In most programming languages, including Python, `assert` is a statement or function that is used to test if a condition is true at some point in the code execution. It is commonly used as a debugging aid to test an assumption about a piece of code as it is being executed, typically during development or testing of an application. 

The assert statement takes an expression and evaluates it to a Boolean value. If the expression is True, then the program continues to execute without any changes. However, if the expression is False, then an `AssertionError` exception is raised, indicating that the assertion has failed and that there is a bug in the code.

Here is an example of using assert in Python:

```python
x = 5
assert x == 5, "x should be 5"
```

In the above example, the `assert` statement is used to test whether the value of variable `x` is equal to 5. If not, the statement would raise an exception and display the message "x should be 5".

The `assert` statement can be useful for debugging code and for spotting problems early in the development process. However, it is generally not recommended to use `assert` for input validation or for testing runtime errors that might be caused by exceptions (such as IOError, IndexError, KeyError, etc.), since `assert` statements can be disabled in a non-debugging build.
what is AWSCRT

AWS Common Runtime (AWSCRT) is a cross-platform, open-source C library developed by Amazon Web Services (AWS) for building fast and scalable client and server applications. It offers a unified low-level library that handles core functionality across a wide range of AWS services. AWSCRT is designed for building high-performance applications that use AWS services in a variety of languages, including Python, Java, Ruby, C++, and others.

AWSCRT offers a set of core libraries that handle the following:

- Clients and data serialization/deserialization for multiple AWS services
- Secure network connectivity with Transport Layer Security (TLS) and server name indication (SNI) support
- Implementation of MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) and AWS IoT support
- Implementation of HTTP2 and WebSocket protocols

All of these libraries are designed to be lightweight, concurrent, and use asynchronous I/O techniques to provide high scalability and performance. AWSCRT also includes tools to help build and manage applications, such as a CMake build system, memory pool manager, and tracing and logging tools.

Overall, AWSCRT is an essential framework for developers who are looking to build AWS services and applications that are high-performing, scalable, and efficient.
how to change AWS support plan back to Basic

You can change your AWS support plan back to Basic from any other plan or current plan that you have subscribed to by following the steps below:

1. Sign in to your AWS Management Console, and open the AWS Support Center.

2. Choose the “Support Plans” option from the left menu.

3. Verify that you are subscribed to the desired plan under the “Current plan” section.

4. Under the “Change plan” section, click on the “Change” or "Update" button depending on the console version you're using.

5. Select the “Basic” plan under the "Select Plan" section and click on the “Next” button.

6. Review the billing and payment details, and click on the “Submit and Change Plan” button to confirm the change.

Note that your AWS support charges may be prorated based on the date of the change. Therefore, it is recommended to review your billing and payment details before confirming the change.

After you have changed your AWS support plan, you will receive an email from AWS confirming the plan change. Once the change is made, you will have access to the Basic support features, including guidance, documentation, whitepapers, and AWS Trusted Advisor checks.
Will AWS continue to charge me after change support plan back to Basic

AWS will only charge you for the duration that you use their support services that aligns with your support plan. Once you have changed your AWS Support plan back to Basic, you will only be charged for Basic support services that you use. 

AWS support bills are charged on a monthly basis and are prorated based on the date of the change. When you change your support plan, AWS support will automatically update your billing and charge you accordingly.

It's important to note that AWS may also charge you for any other usage costs that you incurred from using any additional AWS services that leverage premium support services such as AWS Trusted Advisor or Infrastructure Event Management (IEM) even though you have downgraded your support plan. Similarly, AWS can charge you for services delivered during the time that you were still on a higher support plan if they were rendered before the support plan change.

Before downgrading your support plan, it's recommended to understand the impact of the downgrade on your budget and services. It is also recommended to consult with an AWS representative or review the AWS support documentation if you have any concerns about billing and other support-related queries.
